g.data-narration: Welcome to confidence limits. Learn how to estimate population parameters with quantifiable uncertainty from sample data.
g.text: Confidence Limits: From Sample to Population

g.data-narration: In data-driven computer science, we rarely have entire populations. Confidence intervals help estimate true parameters from samples.
g.text: In CS, we work with samples: A/B tests, ML models, server performance.
Question: How well does sample mean estimate true population mean?
Answer: Confidence intervals provide a range of plausible values!

g.data-narration: A point estimate gives one number. A confidence interval adds a margin of error, creating a range around that estimate.
A 95% confidence level means if we repeated sampling many times, 95% of intervals would contain the true parameter.
g.text: The confidence level (90%, 95%, 99%) is NOT a probability for YOUR interval.
It's the LONG-RUN success rate of the METHOD:

g.data-narration: The confidence interval formula: Point Estimate ± Margin of Error. The MOE captures uncertainty in our estimate.
Three factors determine MOE: larger samples reduce it, more variability increases it, higher confidence requires wider intervals.
g.text: Three Factors Affecting Margin of Error

g.data-narration: Critical values from Z or t-distributions determine how many standard errors to capture the central confidence area.
g.text: Use Z-distribution when σ (population std) is KNOWN
Use t-distribution when σ is UNKNOWN (use s)

g.data-narration: Standard Error (SE = s/√n) estimates variability of the sampling distribution of the sample mean.
g.text: Estimates variability of the sampling distribution of x̄
Sample std dev divided by √sample size

g.data-narration: The complete confidence interval for a mean uses t-distribution: x̄ ± t*ₙ₋₁ × (s/√n) with n-1 degrees of freedom.
The t-distribution has heavier tails than normal distribution. For n≥30, it approximates Z closely.
g.text: • Accounts for extra uncertainty from using s

g.data-narration: Let's work through an example: estimating mean server response time. Sample: 25 requests, find 95% CI.
g.text: A computer scientist wants to estimate mean response time of a cloud API.
Given: n = 25 | x̄ = 118 ms | s = 20 ms | Confidence = 95%

g.data-narration: Step 1: Find degrees of freedom (24). Step 2: Look up t* for 95% confidence with df=24, which is 2.064.
Margin of Error = t* × SE = 2.064 × 4 = 8.26 ms. Interval: 118 ± 8.26 gives 109.74 to 126.26 ms.
g.text: Calculate Margin of Error and Interval

g.data-narration: The confidence interval [109.74, 126.26] is shown graphically with the sample mean at center and margin of error extending equally.
Interpretation: We are 95% confident the true mean response time is between 109.74 and 126.26 milliseconds.
g.text: "We are 95% confident that the true mean response time

g.data-narration: Key takeaways: Confidence intervals = Point Estimate ± MOE. MOE depends on sample size, variability, and confidence level.
g.text: • CI = Point Estimate ± Margin of Error
Essential for hypothesis testing, A/B tests, ML model evaluation
